nodes.name: Fraction arithmetic
nodes.description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide numeric fractions, including finding common denominators and simplifying results.

nodes.name: Exponent rules
nodes.description: Apply product, quotient, power, zero, and negative exponent rules to simplify expressions.

nodes.name: Polynomial operations
nodes.description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; expand products and collect like terms.

nodes.name: Factoring polynomials
nodes.description: Factor polynomials using common factors, difference of squares, and quadratic trinomial patterns.

nodes.name: Solving linear equations
nodes.description: Solve one-variable linear equations by isolating the unknown with inverse operations.

nodes.name: Solving quadratic equations
nodes.description: Solve quadratic equations by factoring, completing the square, or the quadratic formula.

nodes.name: Radicals and rational exponents
nodes.description: Convert between radical and rational-exponent notation and simplify radical expressions.

nodes.name: Simplifying rational expressions
nodes.description: Simplify rational and compound algebraic expressions by factoring and canceling common factors.

nodes.name: Function notation and evaluation
nodes.description: Interpret and evaluate functions written as f(x), including evaluation at expressions.

nodes.name: Domain and range
nodes.description: Determine the set of valid inputs and possible outputs of a function.

nodes.name: Reading function graphs
nodes.description: Read values, intercepts, slopes, and qualitative behavior from the graph of a function.
canonical_examples: From a graph, f(2) = 4 and f is increasing on (0, 3)

nodes.name: Function composition
nodes.description: Build and evaluate composite functions f(g(x)) and decompose a function into inner and outer parts.
canonical_examples: If f(x) = x^2 and g(x) = 3x + 1 then f(g(x)) = (3x + 1)^2
h(x) = sin(x^2) has outer sin(u) and inner u = x^2

nodes.name: Inverse functions
nodes.description: Find and verify inverse functions and understand their graphs as reflections over y = x.

nodes.name: Graph transformations
nodes.description: Predict how shifts, stretches, and reflections transform the graph of a function.
canonical_examples: g(x) = (x - 2)^2 + 1 shifts x^2 right 2 and up 1

nodes.name: Exponential and logarithmic functions
nodes.description: Work with exponential and logarithmic functions, including the inverse relationship between them.

nodes.name: Trigonometric functions and the unit circle
nodes.description: Evaluate sine, cosine, and tangent at standard angles using the unit circle.

nodes.name: Basic trig identities
nodes.description: Use fundamental identities such as sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1 to rewrite expressions.

nodes.name: Limit intuition and notation
nodes.description: Describe the limiting value of a function as inputs approach a point, from graphs and tables.
canonical_examples: lim as x approaches 2 of x^2 is 4

nodes.name: Computing limits with limit laws
nodes.description: Compute limits algebraically using limit laws and by simplifying indeterminate forms.

nodes.name: Continuity
nodes.description: Determine where a function is continuous and classify discontinuities.
canonical_examples: (x^2 - 9)/(x - 3) has a removable discontinuity at x = 3

nodes.name: Average vs. instantaneous rate of change
nodes.description: Distinguish average rate of change (secant slope) from instantaneous rate of change (tangent slope).
canonical_examples: Average rate of x^2 on [1, 3] is (9 - 1)/(3 - 1) = 4

nodes.name: Derivative as a limit
nodes.description: Define the derivative as the limit of difference quotients.
canonical_examples: f'(x) = lim as h approaches 0 of (f(x + h) - f(x))/h
For f(x) = x^2 the definition gives f'(x) = 2x

nodes.name: Power rule
nodes.description: Differentiate power functions using d/dx x^n = n x^(n-1).

nodes.name: Linearity of differentiation
nodes.description: Differentiate sums, differences, and constant multiples term by term.

nodes.name: Product and quotient rules
nodes.description: Differentiate products and quotients using the product and quotient rules.

nodes.name: Derivatives of trig functions
nodes.description: Differentiate sine, cosine, and tangent.

nodes.name: Derivatives of exponential and log functions
nodes.description: Differentiate exponential and logarithmic functions.

nodes.name: Chain rule
nodes.description: Differentiate composite functions: the derivative of f(g(x)) is f'(g(x)) times g'(x).

nodes.name: Differentials and Leibniz notation
nodes.description: Relate du and dx through du = u'(x) dx and use Leibniz notation fluently.
canonical_examples: If u = x^2 then du = 2x dx

nodes.name: Implicit differentiation
nodes.description: Differentiate relations that define y implicitly, applying the chain rule to y-terms.

nodes.name: Area under a curve as accumulation
nodes.description: Interpret the area between a curve and the x-axis as an accumulated quantity.
canonical_examples: Area under v(t) on [0, 4] gives distance traveled

nodes.name: Riemann sums
nodes.description: Approximate area under a curve with sums of rectangle areas that improve with finer partitions.
canonical_examples: Left sum of x^2 on [0, 1] with 4 rectangles: (0 + 1/16 + 4/16 + 9/16) * (1/4)

nodes.name: The definite integral
nodes.description: Define the definite integral as the limit of Riemann sums; interpret its sign and notation.
canonical_examples: integral from 0 to 1 of x^2 dx = 1/3

nodes.name: Antiderivatives
nodes.description: Find functions whose derivative is a given function, reversing differentiation rules.

nodes.name: Indefinite integrals and +C
nodes.description: Use indefinite integral notation and include the constant of integration.

nodes.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
nodes.description: Connect derivatives and definite integrals: evaluate definite integrals using antiderivatives.

nodes.name: Basic integration rules and table
nodes.description: Integrate term by term using the reverse power rule and the basic table of trig and exponential integrals.
canonical_examples: integral of (3x^2 + cos(x)) dx = x^3 + sin(x) + C

nodes.name: Recognizing composite structure in integrands
nodes.description: Spot when an integrand contains a composite function together with a multiple of its inner derivative.
canonical_examples: In integral of 2x cos(x^2) dx, the factor 2x is the derivative of the inner x^2

nodes.name: U-substitution (indefinite integrals)
nodes.description: Evaluate integrals by substituting u for an inner function and converting dx to du, reversing the chain rule.
canonical_examples: integral of 2x cos(x^2) dx = sin(x^2) + C via u = x^2

nodes.name: U-substitution with definite integrals
nodes.description: Apply u-substitution to definite integrals, converting the bounds to u-values or back-substituting before evaluating.
canonical_examples: integral from 0 to 1 of 2x e^(x^2) dx = e - 1 via u = x^2 with bounds 0 to 1

edges.rationale: Simplifying rational expressions reuses fraction arithmetic with variables.
Canceling and combining terms requires exponent manipulation.
Rational exponents generalize integer exponent rules.
Factoring reverses polynomial multiplication.
Factoring is the primary method for solving quadratics.
Each factored root reduces to a linear equation.
Canceling common factors requires factoring numerator and denominator.
Domain and range are properties of functions expressed in function notation.
Graphs are read as values of f(x) at inputs x.
Composition substitutes one function's output into another's notation.
Inverses are verified through composition: f(f^-1(x)) = x.
Transformations are described relative to a readable base graph.
Exponential and log functions are used and evaluated as functions.
Exponential functions build directly on exponent arithmetic.
Trig functions are evaluated with function notation on angle inputs.
Identities relate the values of the basic trig functions.
Limit intuition is built by reading function behavior from graphs.
Limit laws formalize the intuitive notion of a limit.
Indeterminate forms are resolved by algebraic simplification.
Continuity is defined by comparing limits with function values.
Rates of change are seen as slopes on graphs.
Slope computations use linear-equation arithmetic.
The derivative formalizes instantaneous rate of change.
The derivative is defined as a limit of difference quotients.
The power rule manipulates exponents, including negative and fractional ones.
The power rule is justified by the limit definition, though often used without it.
Term-by-term differentiation applies the power rule to each term.
Product and quotient rules extend basic differentiation fluency.
Differentiating trig functions requires knowing the functions themselves.
Trig derivatives appear inside sums and constant multiples.
Differentiating e^x and ln(x) requires the functions and their properties.
Exponential and log derivatives are used within linear combinations.
The chain rule differentiates composite functions; you must see the composition first.
Chain rule practice leans on differentiating the outer power or basic function.
Mixed problems combine chain rule with product and quotient rules.
Computing du = u'(x) dx requires differentiating u.
Differentials inherit meaning from the limit definition of the derivative.
Implicit differentiation applies the chain rule to y-terms.
Accumulated area is interpreted from the graph of the function.
Riemann sums approximate the area concept with rectangles.
Evaluating sums of rectangle areas uses polynomial arithmetic.
The definite integral is the limit of Riemann sums.
Antiderivatives reverse the power rule.
Antidifferentiation is applied term by term.
Indefinite integral notation names the family of antiderivatives.
The FTC evaluates definite integrals.
The FTC uses antiderivatives to evaluate integrals.
Table integration is expressed in indefinite integral notation.
The trig part of the integral table reverses trig derivatives.
The exponential part of the integral table reverses those derivatives.
Recognizing inner and outer functions is a composition skill.
Spotting an inner derivative in the integrand mirrors the chain rule.
Choosing u requires seeing the composite structure.
Converting dx to du uses du = u'(x) dx.
After substitution the remaining integral is evaluated from the basic table.
U-substitution reverses the chain rule.
Definite u-substitution builds on the indefinite technique.
Evaluating with new bounds uses the FTC.
